title: Latent degradation state features via simple per-stint filter
summary: Recent F1 research argues degradation is better represented as a latent stochastic state with pit-stop resets than as a fixed deterministic wear curve.
rationale: Recent F1 research argues degradation is better represented as a latent stochastic state with pit-stop resets than as a fixed deterministic wear curve. That matches this competition, where raw `TyreLife` and `Cumulative_Degradation` likely miss hidden pace-collapse dynamics and driver-specific tyre management.
implementation_hint: Build lightweight per-driver-per-race filtered states in lap order: exponentially updated latent pace loss, latent degradation slope, shock term from sudden lap-time delta jumps, and reset-aware residuals after pit stops. Feed only one-step-behind filtered values, never smoothed full-stint estimates. Compare a simple Kalman/EMA-style filter to your current handcrafted window heuristics.
expected_effect: Should sharpen decision boundaries near the true pit cliff while remaining interpretable and cheap.
risk: The biggest failure mode is accidental smoothing with future laps; noisy lap times from traffic or weather may contaminate the latent state unless clipped or robustified.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
